title: Iran Consolidates Control of Hormuz as UK Eases Russian Oil Sanctions
subhead: Tehran establishes multi-tiered checkpoint system for Strait transits while Britain quietly allows Russian refined products amid global fuel shortages triggered by the Iran war.

sections.heading: Iran Enforces Checkpoint System for Hormuz Transits
sections.body:
Iran has established a multi-tiered control system for vessels seeking to transit the Strait of Hormuz, according to a Reuters investigation published Wednesday. The system involves island checkpoints, diplomatic coordination with flag states, and in some cases transit fees negotiated through back channels.

The enforcement mechanism has emerged as some shippers and governments choose to comply with Iran's controls rather than risk being targeted. The US has warned against complying with Iranian requirements, but with global oil supplies severely constrained, some nations are calculating that coordination with Tehran is preferable to indefinite delays.

Two Chinese supertankers carrying 4 million barrels of crude exited the strait after waiting two months, along with a South Korean tanker that passed through an Iran-approved route after Seoul-Tehran coordination. About 19 non-Iranian tankers have successfully transited the strait since March, with Greek firm Dynacom using tactics like disabling transponders and sailing at night to avoid attacks.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's checkpoint system represents a durable control mechanism that persists even during ceasefire periods. States that coordinate with Tehran gain transit access while those relying solely on US naval protection face indefinite delays, creating incentives for bilateral arrangements with Iran.

sections.heading: UK Issues Sanctions Waivers for Russian Refined Products
sections.body:
Britain issued a general trade license on May 20 allowing imports of diesel and jet fuel refined abroad from Russian crude, marking a significant easing of sanctions imposed after the 2022 Ukraine invasion. The license is of indefinite duration and will be periodically reviewed, according to the Department of Business and Trade.

The government also issued a temporary license loosening sanctions on LNG from certain Russian plants. The move follows a similar US waiver extension for Russian oil cargoes, reflecting the strain on fuel supplies caused by the Hormuz closure.

Treasury minister Dan Tomlinson defended the decision as "protecting the UK national interest" given the extreme impact of the Iran conflict on fuel availability. Opposition leader Kemi Badenoch denounced the move, calling out what she described as hypocrisy after 18 months of "standing up to Putin." The EU criticized the US waiver extension at G7 finance ministers meetings.
sections.whyItMatters: Western sanctions architecture is buckling under the combined pressure of the Hormuz closure and domestic fuel price spikes. The UK and US waivers create a two-tier system where Russian crude processed in third countries like India flows freely, undermining the sanctions regime's credibility.

sections.heading: Gulf States Accelerate Overland Pipeline Corridors
sections.body:
Gulf monarchies are fast-tracking pipeline and logistics deals to create an overland Levant-Gulf corridor that bypasses Hormuz entirely. The effort has accelerated since the strait closure began in February, fundamentally reshaping regional energy infrastructure planning.

The UAE's new 360km pipeline to Fujairah on the Gulf of Oman is now 50% complete, ADNOC CEO Sultan Al Jaber confirmed Wednesday. The pipeline, expected operational in 2027, will double the UAE's Hormuz-bypass capacity. Saudi Aramco is leaning on its existing East-West pipeline to Yanbu on the Red Sea.

Traffic at Gulf of Oman ports has surged from 100 trucks per day before the conflict to 7,000 trucks per day, according to the Wall Street Journal. Egypt and Syria signed memoranda in January for Egyptian gas exports to Syria, positioning Cairo as a supplier into a Levant corridor. Syria is being recast as a transit state, with TotalEnergies, QatarEnergy and ConocoPhillips signing an offshore exploration deal near Latakia.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz blockade has accelerated infrastructure projects that reduce Gulf dependence on the strait. While overland routes cannot fully replace maritime capacity, they absorb shocks and create lasting economic integration between Gulf producers and Levant states.

sections.heading: Russia Launches Major Nuclear Exercise With 65,000 Troops
sections.body:
Russia began its largest nuclear exercises in years on May 19 without prior notice, mobilizing 65,000 troops, 200 missile launchers, 140 aircraft, 73 surface vessels and 13 submarines including eight strategic nuclear submarines. The three-day drill includes live ballistic and cruise missile launches inside Russia.

The exercise explicitly integrates Belarusian launch sites for the first time, with Minsk announcing parallel drills practicing nuclear munitions delivery from Belarusian territory. Russia has stationed its Oreshnik intermediate-range ballistic missile in Belarus since 2025, capable of conventional or nuclear warheads.

The timing coincides with Vladimir Putin's Beijing visit and follows a successful Sarmat ICBM test on May 12 after earlier failures. Analysts at the Institute for the Study of War assess the drills aim to influence NATO decision-making and potentially draw Ukrainian reserves away from the front ahead of a planned Russian summer offensive.
sections.whyItMatters: The unannounced exercise signals nuclear readiness during a period of Western focus on the Iran conflict. Belarus integration extends Russia's nuclear posture deeper into Europe, complicating NATO's eastern flank planning.

sections.heading: China-Japan Rare Earth Tensions Persist Despite Partial Recovery
sections.body:
China's exports of rare earth permanent magnets to Japan recovered only modestly in April, rising 2.5% from March but only partially offsetting the 17.3% plunge recorded in March. Japanese firms are warning of "severe" shortages as a diplomatic row between Beijing and Tokyo grinds on.

The dispute erupted in November when Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi suggested a hypothetical attack on Taiwan could constitute an "existential threat" to Japan. Beijing responded by restricting economic ties, including a January ban on "dual-use" exports to Japan, a category that can include rare earth elements.

China's Ministry of Commerce said Wednesday it will work with the US on "reasonable" concerns regarding rare earth export controls, defending its restrictions as legitimate and lawful. The White House announced after the Trump-Xi summit that China promised to "address US concerns" over rare earth supply chain shortages.
sections.whyItMatters: Rare earth controls have become a selective pressure tool in Beijing's diplomatic toolkit. Japan faces ongoing supply pressure while the US secured a summit commitment for supply chain relief, revealing how Beijing differentiates treatment based on strategic priorities.

sections.heading: Indonesia Centralizes Commodity Export Control
sections.body:
Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto announced a sweeping regulation on May 20 requiring state-owned enterprises to become the sole exporters of key commodities including coal, palm oil, and ferroalloys. The move is designed to boost state revenues and crack down on illicit export practices.

"Indonesia's natural resources belong to the Indonesian people," Prabowo told Parliament. "Therefore, the state has the right to know in detail how those resources are sold abroad." Exporters will have a three-month transition period before state enterprises take control.

Indonesia is the world's largest nickel producer and top coal exporter. The announcement sent nickel prices higher on the London Metal Exchange. Analysts warn the controls could distort global markets and create domestic monopolies rather than solving underlying issues.
sections.whyItMatters: A major commodity exporter is centralizing control over strategic resources at a time of global supply chain stress. The move could tighten nickel supplies critical for EV batteries and create pricing volatility as state entities replace private exporters.

sections.heading: Türkiye Proposes NATO Fuel Pipeline for Eastern Flank
sections.body:
Türkiye submitted a proposal to NATO for a military fuel pipeline project designed to strengthen energy security on the alliance's eastern flank, National Defense Ministry officials announced Wednesday. The pipeline would be financed through NATO's common funds.

Defense officials highlighted the project's strategic importance given the Hormuz crisis, aiming to reduce NATO's reliance on maritime fuel transport while improving interoperability. The ministry said the project is "five times more cost-effective" than alternatives and could become operational faster.

The issue has been discussed since March when Eastern European allies pushed the project ahead of NATO's Ankara summit. NATO has also assigned two additional Patriot systems to Türkiye following regional conflicts, with Germany rotating one system into place by June.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye is positioning itself as essential to NATO's eastern flank logistics at a time when US force posture in Europe is being reassessed. An overland fuel pipeline would reduce vulnerability to maritime disruptions in the Mediterranean and Black Sea.

bottomLine: Iran's consolidation of Hormuz transit controls is creating durable leverage that persists beyond active hostilities, incentivizing states to negotiate bilateral arrangements with Tehran. Meanwhile, Western sanctions on Russian oil are eroding under the pressure of fuel shortages, with the UK and US both issuing waivers that effectively restore Russian product flows through third-country refining. Gulf states are responding with accelerated infrastructure investments in overland corridors that will permanently reduce Hormuz dependence, while Russia flexes nuclear capabilities during Western distraction and Indonesia centralizes control over strategic commodity exports. The global energy map is being reshaped by the convergence of the Iran conflict, sanctions fatigue, and resource nationalism.
sources: Reuters, The National, SCMP, OilPrice, Defense News, Pipeline Journal, Anadolu Agency, Strait Times, Bloomberg, MSN, Kyiv Independent, Al Jazeera, Times of Israel
